behaviors.description: Files placed inside subdirectories of the watched root are processed, not only files in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The watcher must actually observe subdirectories. Mocking the watcher to emit subdirectory files would pass even if the real watcher library isn't configured to watch nested paths. Testing requires a real filesystem with subdirectories and a real watcher to verify it discovers them.
behaviors.test_description: Create a watched root directory with a nested subdirectory structure. Write a test file to a subdirectory and verify the daemon detects and processes it. Confirm nested subdirectories (not just direct children) are also observed.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: The dedup algorithm itself is testable at Unit level, but verifying it actually prevents duplicate processing in the daemon's event handling pipeline requires Integration. A Unit mock could pass while the daemon still processes both events if the dedup cache isn't wired into the event loop correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Simulate two processing requests for the same file within the configured time window (e.g., 100ms). Verify the daemon processes the file only once. Test with overlapping request arrival times to ensure the cache operates correctly under realistic conditions.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Multiple filesystem events are real OS behavior that mocks wouldn't reliably emit. Even if you script a mock watcher to emit multiple events, the test doesn't verify the daemon's real event handling loop integrates deduplication correctly. Integration testing ensures the dedup logic operates in the actual event pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Watch a temporary directory and write a file that may trigger multiple filesystem watcher events (OS-dependent; e.g., write with chunks or rapid overwrites). Verify the daemon processes the file exactly once despite potentially receiving multiple arrival notifications.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: The watcher's error handling and recovery behavior only manifest with real filesystem removal. A mock wouldn't trigger the error that forces the recovery path. Real filesystem operations reveal integration issues like timeout handling, re-establishment logic, and error propagation.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon watching a temporary directory. While running, remove the directory. Verify the daemon detects the removal (via watcher error or timeout). Recreate the directory. Verify the daemon resumes watching new files without restart or manual intervention.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Filesystem readdir order is not guaranteed. Mocking readdir to return sorted files would pass even if the daemon never calls a sort function. Real filesystem with multiple files having distinct mtimes reveals whether the daemon actually sorts or just processes in filesystem order.
behaviors.test_description: Write multiple files to the watched directory with creation timestamps differing by at least 1 second. Verify the daemon processes them in ascending timestamp order (oldest first). Test with 5+ files and ensure the order is consistent across multiple runs.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Partial file writes and in-progress I/O cannot be realistically mocked. Mocking file sizes or mtimes would pass even if the detection logic is incorrect. Real filesystem with staged writes and genuine file state changes is necessary to test that the daemon detects incomplete files and waits for stabilization.
behaviors.test_description: Write a file to the watched directory in stages (e.g., write initial content, trigger watcher event before final chunk arrives). Verify the daemon skips the incomplete file and processes it only after writing completes. Test file size stability detection or mtime checks to ensure the daemon doesn't race incomplete writes.
